one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive SoK that gives GenAI-content watermarking a unified cryptographic vocabulary -- a weakest-to-strongest property hierarchy (empirical quality -> distortion-free -> undetectable), a removal/forgery/extraction threat taxonomy, and property-anchored surveys of text and image schemes -- while surfacing key tensions (unforgeability vs robustness, watermarks as invalid copyright proof, and new privacy risks).
key_finding: Watermarking is positioned as more reliable than post-hoc detection, whose empirical error rates typically do not fall below 10^-3 and which cannot furnish theoretical false-positive guarantees or handle out-of-distribution content. Within the property hierarchy, undetectability is the strongest quality notion: it implies quality is preserved under ANY efficiently computable metric (even across many generations) and implies detection/decoding/attribution keys cannot be learned. Several tensions/impossibilities are systematized: unforgeability is fundamentally INCOMPATIBLE with robustness (a robust detector lets an attacker lightly edit a genuine generation and falsely attribute it), resolved by keeping Attribute/ak separate from Detect/dtk; statistical undetectability is impossible (only computational is achievable, per Christ et al.); and a content-entropy/randomness condition is necessary for any detectable-yet-undetectable text watermark. Among surveyed schemes, pseudorandom error-correcting codes (Christ & Gunn) yield the first LM watermark that is simultaneously undetectable and robust to a constant fraction of token substitutions, though PRCs are noted as not yet implemented in a practical LLM scheme. A flagged open problem: watermarks do NOT constitute valid proof-of-generation for copyright, because for many schemes a malicious model owner can manufacture a fake key under which arbitrary user content appears watermarked; and undetectable/distortion-free watermarks introduce new deanonymization/privacy threats.
core_contribution: A Systematization-of-Knowledge (SoK) paper on watermarking the OUTPUTS of generative-AI models (text, image, audio, video) for the purpose of distinguishing AI-generated from human content. Its OWN contributions (as opposed to the schemes it surveys) are: (1) a unified formal syntax for a watermarking scheme, factoring it into WatermarkM_gk (generation), Detect_dtk, Decode_dck, and Attribute_ak, with explicitly separate generation/detection/decoding/attribution keys; (2) a property hierarchy for output quality presented weakest-to-strongest -- empirical quality validation, low-distortion (Def 3.1), distortion-freeness (Def 3.2, computational vs statistical), and undetectability (Def 3.3) -- plus formally defined false-positive rate (Def 3.4), robustness w.r.t. a channel/entropy property (Def 3.5), unforgeability (Def 3.6), multi-bit message embedding, and computational efficiency; (3) a structured threat-model taxonomy separating attack objectives (removal, forgery/spoofing, secret-key extraction) from adversary knowledge/capability dimensions; (4) a systematization of empirical evaluation methodology and attack families; (5) a property-anchored survey of representative schemes (Table 1) plus a review of the regulatory/industry policy landscape and a list of open problems. It is a survey/systematization, NOT a new-method or empirical-benchmark paper.
threat_model: The paper systematizes threat models rather than mounting an attack. It identifies two primary adversarial objectives -- watermark REMOVAL (perturb content so Detect returns false or Decode returns the wrong message while preserving quality) and FORGERY/spoofing (make non-watermarked content be flagged as watermarked, so it can be falsely attributed to a target model) -- plus a harder tertiary objective, secret-key EXTRACTION. It enumerates adversary-capability dimensions: generator-oracle access, quantity of available watermarked / non-watermarked content, white-box model access, non-watermarked-generator access, chosen-key oracle access, verifier-feedback granularity (true/false vs probability score), verifier-oracle query budget, and surrogate-model access. For robustness evaluation it categorizes removal attacks into edit attacks (token deletion/synonym substitution, image resize/noise), regeneration attacks (paraphrase/summarize models for text; denoising-autoencoder/diffusion re-encode for images), and downsampling/deletion attacks (the Emoji / Pineapple attack that inserts then strips symbols to break k-gram-based watermarks).
limitations: This is a qualitative SoK, not an empirical study: its cross-scheme comparison (Table 1) uses shaded circles rather than numbers, so it offers no reproducible head-to-head benchmark of the surveyed schemes. Scope is deliberately narrowed to detecting GenAI OUTPUT content; model watermarking and dataset watermarking are only briefly discussed (Section 7). The formal treatment and scheme coverage are strongest for text and image; the authors acknowledge that video and audio generative-content watermarking have 'relatively few studies' and lean on frame-wise/traditional techniques. Being an arXiv-heavy, fast-moving field, it is a 2024/2025 snapshot. Several cited robustness/quality claims of surveyed schemes are taken from those works' own (sometimes single-response) evaluations, which the SoK critiques but does not independently re-run.

datasets_used: None -- this is a survey/SoK with no new experiments; instead it systematizes 100+ peer-reviewed papers plus regulatory and industry documents
quantitative_results: Reviewed over 100 peer-reviewed papers from the early 2000s to the 2020s across security (USENIX Security, IEEE S&P), AI (NeurIPS, ICML), and multimedia (CVPR, ACM Multimedia) venues (paper's stated methodology scope)

Post-hoc AI-content detectors' reported empirical error rates typically do not fall below 10^-3, and cannot provide theoretical false-positive guarantees (cited limitation motivating watermarking, not the paper's own experiment)
Fixed low-FPR evaluation is standard practice, e.g. detection performance reported at a 0.1% false-positive rate

SynthID is cited as the only surveyed scheme whose empirical quality test approaches production scale, using human evaluation over 5 categories: grammar, relevance, correctness, helpfulness, and overall quality
Green-Red watermark detection z-metric stated as z = (|s|_G - gamma*T) / sqrt(T*gamma*(1-gamma)), where T is text length, |s|_G is green-token count, gamma the target green ratio
baselines_compared: Text schemes systematized (Table 1, cited work not the paper's own methods): Green-Red Watermark [26,27] (Kirchenbauer et al. / fixed-partition), Gumbel Watermark [25] (Aaronson) and Kuditipudi et al. [68] distortion-free variant, Undetectable Watermark [28] (Christ et al.) and Fairoze et al. [75] unforgeable public attribution, Pseudorandom Error-correcting Codes / PRC [73] (Christ & Gunn), Semantic Sentence watermark SemStamp [112] and k-SemStamp [122]

Image schemes systematized: Stable Signature [113] (LDM decoder fine-tuning), Tree-Ring [29] (Fourier-domain latent, zero-bit), Gaussian Shading [69] (fixed-quadrant latent, claims 'lossless' single-image), PRC Watermark [30] (undetectable latent-diffusion scheme)
Contrast class: post-hoc detection -- zero-shot (DetectGPT [17], Fast-DetectGPT [18], Binoculars [20]) and training-based (RADAR [19]), and deprecated OpenAI AI text classifier [24]
